speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Lily Frank Grossman. Sorry I had to interrupt you. You started to say the gendarmes came, and they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted my father declared a spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they think your father was a spy?

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't mean anything. It was just because you were Jewish. Nothing else. And a well-doing family. We were very good friends together. Still, they came because that was their order to came. And they didn't find anything. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: --could plant it if they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away my father to jail, to Miskolc. And that time, if you have a family member to jail, you should say goodbye because they were put in a camp in Hungary. And then, maybe you never saw them again. My brother, Leslie, was in Munka Tabor labor camp. But he was 21 years old, so he was in-- the usual, 21 years old, everybody went for soldier. But he wasn't a soldier. He was in the labor camp. But he was in Hungary. He wasn't taken out, over the border. And what happened, the jail bought wholesale meat from us every week. And I told the guard, oh, you'll get very good meat from us. But maybe we have some favor from you if we are going to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a stupid joke. So my father was over there. And that guard was such a good-- he carried, for my father, bread or salami. And he always said, what is my father doing? And we have a lawyer, the best lawyer, no Jewish lawyer, the best lawyer of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just gave them money, uncounted, to helping us. And all of the sudden, the 1st of May, the communist biggest national holiday, they free my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What holiday was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 1st. That is the Red Day for the Russians and the whole world's communists-- it-- whatever they are, May 1st. My father was [INAUDIBLE] a Russian [INAUDIBLE] taken, but they freed him May the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: May the 1st.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came home. But May the 4th they told us, if we are working in agriculture, we are not taken out from the country, from Hungary. No deportation, nothing. But-- but really, we didn't believe. It was-- OK, it was-- like called [NON-ENGLISH], like Ontario, Quebec, Manitoba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Province.

speaker: interviewee
text: Provinces, but small, you know? But just wondering about how small it was, Hungary by square kilometer or square feet-- the same size like Lake Ontario. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was small. The fifth province from us was taken already to Auschwitz. I mean, we don't know where. The brick factory, the ghetto, the brick factory and was taken in cattle wagons. We said no, [NON-ENGLISH] won't take us. They like the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You really believed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. Yeah, sure we believed it. I don't know why. I really don't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: We let us-- to go. And they said, Israel-- why did you let yourself to do it? First of all, all the men was taken from the village. Who stayed home? The old women, the middle-aged women, and children, or anybody over 75 or 80. Do you know why your father had never been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father just came home from the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that he was labor camp, but it was just light things to do. Sometimes he came home from the weekend. So that-- that went slowly, step by step by step. We started April 1st, I think, to wear the yellow star. And we have a post office in the end of the village. So if I wanted to go to the post office, I have to walk all over the village. And to go with a yellow star, stamped like that in the village, that was a terrible thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You won't believe it. We have a very nice house, nicely furnished, everything. And soldier was in the village. They were just Hungarian soldiers-- was located there. I don't know why, but it was. Maybe they-- Miskolc was full, you know? The [NON-ENGLISH]. Inn? Like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the inn. Where the soldiers was. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: The barracks? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they were in-- in Felsozsolca, close to Miskolc. So we got two officers. [INAUDIBLE], they're called. It's a good, a lieutenant or whatever they're called. And one of them, called Steiner, it's a Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even-- even his nose was like that-- tall guy. And I have to go to the post office, and I started to-- to cry. And I said, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said you know what? You are going. And I am going with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was life threatening for him. Maybe for me, also. He came with me. And he was just whispering to me, chin up. Smile. Say, good morning, good afternoon. And walk and walk. That was a support for me, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was a reporter at the newspaper. Maybe he was very human, a humanitarian. I don't know. Not the other officer. He said everything for him-- you crazy. You crazy. But he wasn't a bad leader. So anyway, we went May the 4th. We packed up our things-- underwear, some dresses, some shoes. We didn't know how long we will be. We didn't know it should be summer or winter clothes and shoes. And food. And my mother, sure, brought her candle holder and some dishes. And we were taken the next village for agriculture-- agriculture work. And we went in a big place, all cement, cold. But it was May, so it doesn't matter. And we went out on the field to work from morning to night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there? And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Digging--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not digging.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hoeing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. The corn and the bean and everything. It was hard, but we were young, so when my mother was slow, I went to help her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next village from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big, big farm, a grove. It's big things, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Munk. And you know what? And-- and [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know him before. But that Munk was a converted Jew. They were converted for Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was converted? The monks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Munks-- Munk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were in a monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-N-K. No. That's the name-- was M-U-N-K. Not 'o.'

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was Mr. And Mrs. Munk, and their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were converted Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And what do you think? They kept up the gendarme-- gendarmes, six or eight gendarmes. They feed them from the-- and when we get to the ghetto, they were taken in the ghetto. We and them were beat up by the gendarmes. You wouldn't believe it. And Mrs. Munk and her beautiful daughter was dying with us in Botten.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just telling you, that was the gendarmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though they were converted.

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept them with-- with pigs-- two, three, big. For the whole year. Bread, corn, everything. So we were over there almost one month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family was together. We eat together. We sleep together. My brother, Leslie, was in his labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other family members with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tibor-- Tibikia, we called. So 1st of June or 2nd of June-- I'm not very sure because they bombed Miskolc that time. So we were packed up in a very big horse wagon-- big, big. And we were taken to Diosgyor, next from Miskolc. A steel factory, very famous, was there at this time. And we were taken to a school, to the ghetto. The same afternoon-- it was a Thursday-- the same afternoon, we were taken by our own friendly gendarmes to a building, which was one room, one kitchen; one room, one kitchen-- about eight or 10 units. And every unit was different villages' gendarmes. That was Felsozsolca. The next was Hejocsabai, The next was Malyi, the Kistokaj, every one of them. And we were taken. Felsozsolca was one group. The Hejocsabai was the other group.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was taken in first. And he didn't come back to our group. He was taken a little further. And whatever we see? He's changing his feet-- left feet, right feet, left-- we didn't know why he is so nervous. What happened? Oh, he-- what was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was beaten up. But we didn't know. My mother was the next. We were a so-called rich or elite family, so they started with us. My mother came out from the room-- we still didn't know what's going on-- on her four.

speaker: interviewee
text: She crawled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: She crawled out. Another place, not close to my father. I was the next. They let me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a young girl. The whole world is mine. So what could be-- happen with me? The next minute they said, remove my shoes. Sit down on the floor. Pull up my knees. Put my hand through. They tied up my hand-- OK? Put through, and they put under my knee a broom, the stick of the broom.

speaker: interviewee
text: They turned me up. And the two gendarmes started with a-- with a stick, a rubber stick. And two others was there-- not gendarmes, civil-- they're called [NON-ENGLISH], like you talk, a KGB in Russia. They beat and beat me, not just my body, but my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soles of your feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sole-- the feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the rubber sticks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they beat and beat me, these two animals. They asked me, where is our jewelry? Where is my dowry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dowry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dowry, whatever. And questions like that. I couldn't say a single word, even if I want, because I was afraid. I mean, I didn't know what my father said, what my mother said. So if I am saying something else, they will be beaten up again. And me, too. And before I went in, it was a well on the yard. It was a big place. And one old man was jumping in the well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was afraid. He saw how the people coming out from different room. But I tell you something-- later, we learned something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the time was over, so they didn't take in my little brother, Tibor. So they forced me to put on my shoes, but my sole was like that, swollen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was blue and green and bleeding all over. So-- but they have to force me to walk, my mother, my father. And that was when we meet again. In the other room, let's say, the Hejocsabai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Toronto, [NON-ENGLISH], who was in the group, and we went together. They helped my mother and me to go because we can't step. But we have to go. They said, do you hear what the Felsozsolca-- the gendarmes doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are screaming like the dog when they're beating the dog. Just about-- they didn't kill them. They didn't beat them. They said, if you want to say something-- where is your money or jewelry-- you say it. If not, not, walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the difference. Don't tell me they just went by order. That depends from the character, of the-- even from the gendarme. That's an old story, and lie, and robbery, and murderer-- the Hungarian people. So we went home. And we were on our stomach. We couldn't go on our back. And the boys-- They let you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, back to this ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto. It was a school. And they, you know, they ask us from the bed sheet, put it in water, and they were turning us like that. And do not forget, the-- the men was beaten and tortured by their private part, OK? And this is unbearable. And they just-- you know, and they took us out. We can't move a step. And they took us out to the latrine, that was the toilet, in this sheet, the wet sheet. Then we went to the brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did anybody nurse you? How did you get better? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing-- the boys. All of the three of us was in a wet sheet. That was the only. My father was taken one more because they were not satisfied. We didn't say anything. They said, you have a [NON-ENGLISH]-- dog nature, my mother and me. Because we didn't want to say anything. We don't know what my father said. So he was taken again. They tortured him again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, when the-- our own gendarmes who beat up us, so-called friend from home, called, now, how are you Mrs. Krakovics? And she said, you know how you left me. Which means, you beat me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're getting better because we were young, still. And we didn't have any other choice. But we still couldn't put on shoes. And we were taken again, away, walking to the brick factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in the brick factory. We were not very long there because we spent a time in the farm and then in the school. So it wasn't a long time. And we got the rain, and the rain was going under us. We were in the mud. We had very few things with us. What could you carry? Don't talk about even straw. Nothing. So we stand up the whole night. So we were leaving there and-- Any idea--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if we get any food, or we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me any dates of the brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was June 11. June 11 when we was taken [NON-ENGLISH], Miskolc. 12,000 Jews was there. We are going to start to go for work. And cattle wagons up there-- it was a hill-- and the first 1,500 persons get together and go up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was taken, the first train transport, Sunday morning, June 11. At the same time, they selected the men to go to labor camp. My father was taken. My brother, who I said-- said, I am 15 years old. I don't want to be a soldier. So he stayed with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came about 12:00 or 1 o'clock. Get up, and go up to the wagons. And I really didn't know that part. We started to go with crazy speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were at the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: 80 persons.

speaker: interviewer
text: 80 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we hardly could sit. Don't talk about to lie down. And we were, you know, shivering to keep the littlest things-- a piece of salami, a piece of soap, some jewelry we had. It was sewn in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the lining of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the lining, or anywhere, because we thought, maybe we'll need some day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you were in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it is end of the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought it was the end of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because what's wrong comes after that? And many, many wrong things come after that. But we thought, this time, this is the worst. One pail in the middle of the cattle wagon for toilet. And you were thinking about 100 times to go because first of all, your place could be taken away from your family. Second, it was men, woman, children there, so you have to go two person, yeah. One is holding something. And it was stinking. And when the train get a quick stop--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jolt.

speaker: interviewee
text: that [NON-ENGLISH] was upside down, just going anywhere. And it was a very hard day. It was a small window with wire. No air, no water, no nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived to Kosice, Kassa. But the whole night the train went. And it stopped and went backward, then further. That was not the most important things to go for that train. So they opened up the wagon, and they brought some pails of water for us. And sure, we started to fight for it because everybody was crazy already for the whole night together in the stink and hot and everything. And a few persons already died. So they just get him or her and throw out. Wondering about the relative, or the husband, or the wife-- what was they-- what were they doing for this time? And some people wanted to throw bread for us, close to the-- Station. The people that were around the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans started-- wanted to shooting them. That didn't shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: They threatened to shoot them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The threatened to shoot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they were to--

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we went again. That was Monday. And Tuesday, I think close to 12 o'clock or so, or morning time, we arrived to Auschwitz. We didn't know where we are, believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the barracks. And we saw people in pajamas. OK. Lining, right? We said, it's not a bad place. Still the morning, and they're walking in pajamas. What's wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the doors was opening. And the [SIGHS] Polish guys, I don't blame them. It's not their fault. It came like hell to us. Get out! Get out from the wagon! They pull everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. Now, who were these Polish guys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- Jewish Polish guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners were getting you off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All over. Prisoners. Always prisoners. The German with dogs was just watching them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But we didn't know who they are. They don't introduce themselves, right? They were pulling people-- they were hitting, get out! Get out!

speaker: interviewee
text: Leave the packages. You will get it later. Don't worry, you will get it later. Men here, women here. Crying. People was separated. The whole thing was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing at that moment with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I tried to save my-- my skin and get out quickly, holding my mother's hand. My little brother was separated at this time. And it was terrible heat, that day in Auschwitz. But we pulled on him a heavy sweater-- maybe you will need. And he was shvitzing, sweating. And we, whatever we could-- underwear, dress, sweaters-- we pulled on because maybe we need. Because we have to leave the-- the packages, the backpack or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the mean time, on the road, the German came and collected the jewelry. You are going to get out one by one. And we are going to search you. If we are going to find money or jewelry, right away, the same spot, we kill you. So whoever has a little things, they give it. They went around with a hat or whatever, and they collected everything. And some people doesn't dare to take out from the lining. And no time for it. And they didn't know, did I do good, or I didn't? Should I give, or should I? Terrible, terrible. So we were standing, the womens and the mens, separate. And what was your-- did your mother say anything to you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was crying for my brother because he was separated. And she said, whenever you have chance, don't forget to contact us. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: She was yelling after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yelling after him. But they stayed. And the women went first to Mengele. Who knows who? White gloves, very nicely dressed, shiny boots man, and his people around with big German dogs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: German Shepherds.

speaker: interviewee
text: German Shepherds, yeah. You are right. And then he has a stick. And he said, right, left, right, left. So we didn't know. We saw only young people goes that side. And that was the minute-- we were five people, my auntie, Olga, the little boy, Tomika, and Mrs. Bergsman, her mother, and mother, and me. And that was the moment when Mengele told the little boy and the grandmother goes that side. And his mother come with us. She was young. She was young. She was a perfect German. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did he speak? Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people say he spoke Hungarian. Did you ever hear him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. No. No. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little boy, when they started going away, screamed after my auntie, his mother, mommy, do you have heart to leave me alone? So my auntie started to talk with Mengele, perfect English. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And told her, [NON-ENGLISH], when my auntie explained, I want to go the other side, with my son. [NON-ENGLISH] means stupid cow. So she went forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take her at that time? She disappeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going to stop here.
